comments.title: Protection on the road
comments.content: My sister and her two children will be taking a long road trip next week.  I tend to worry excessively about that kind of thing, and I’d like to do something to help ensure a safe trip.  I have my sister’s blanket permission to perform magical workings on her and her children’s behalf, so I think I’m good on the consent aspect.  Since Mithras has already shown himself quite capable of helping me while on the road (I’m certain he saved me from a car crash last year), I’ll be asking for his protection.  But are there any other magical/occult recommendations that you or the commentariat can make for a safe road trip/vacation?

replies.title: Re: Protection on the road
replies.content: Sure. One very simple thing that I learned years ago from an older occultist, is to visualize the car as though it's lit up by a spotlight. A lot of accidents happen because the other person doesn't notice the car they're about to hit. Visualizing the car as lit up and especially visible appears to help with that.

replies.title: Re: Protection on the road
replies.content: Is this basically the opposite of the kind of invisibility working found in (for example) Circles of Power? You did mention in that book that it can make things awkward on busy roads...

replies.title: Re: Protection on the road
replies.content: Yes, exactly. It's a simple visibility working. There are more complex versions, of course, but I don't recommend those unless you want every single person you pass to stare at you!

replies.title: Re: Protection on the road
replies.content: Or, in my case, you are naturally invisible! Being unnoticeable is annoying, and very, very dangerous in a lot of cases...

replies.title: Re: Protection on the road
replies.content: Try the spotlight visualization on yourself and see what results you get.

replies.title: Re: Protection on the road
replies.content: At the risk of sounding like a real noob, how long should I perform the visualization?  And should I do it for each day of the trip?

replies.title: Re: Protection on the road
replies.content: A few minutes is fine -- just until you have a clear sense of the car brightly illuminated -- and yes, you can do it daily.

replies.title: Re: Protection on the road
replies.content: Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Protection on the road
replies.content: Could this be turned from "I'm super visible" to "I'm invisible" at will, without having the problem of conflicting will? For example by turning off the spotlight and turning on a "spotdark" to be unnoticed? Asking as someone who likes to be unnoticed in crowds.

replies.title: Re: Protection on the road
replies.content: That's challenging, and takes practice, but you can subsume both under a single intention -- "I choose how visible I am in each situation" -- so it doesn't necessarily result in a conflict of will.

replies.title: Re: Protection on the road
replies.content:
Could I achieve this by focusing the visualisation on the spotlight itself with a Light/Dark control on it's surface?

I do an SOP daily and I'm a student of the DMH. Would there be an aspect of that, or the symbolism of the wheel of life, which could be applied to the same goal?

Thanks

2) Yes in both cases. Put some time into meditating on that issue with the six directions in mind, and then with the Wheel of Life, and see what you can work out.

comments.title: For the commentariat
comments.content: A few MMs ago, I asked JMG if my practice would be considered henotheism.  The answer is “no,” so I’m hoping that someone in the commentariat can help me come up with a label for the kind polytheism that I do practice.  Basically, it’d be a label for a polytheist who has one primary Deity that receives the highest form of worship (latria, to use the Catholic term), plus other Deities and spiritual beings that receive a lesser form of worship (dulia, in this case).  Can anyone recommend a good label for that?

replies.title: Re: For the commentariat
replies.content: I'd be very interested to hear whether anyone knows a word for it! It's a very common religious approach worldwide, and ought to have a name.

replies.title: Re: For the commentariat
replies.content:
Poking around wikipedia for a few minutes didn't suggest an easy answer (no surprise), so maybe a neologism? "Hierotheism" doesn't make much sense literally, but is easier on the eyes and tongue than "hierarchitheism", the intention being to stress the ranked nature of the different kinds of worship. Perhaps "oligotheism" for "worship of a few gods" (though your definition doesn't necessarily imply a small number of beings receiving dulia).

Or, if you prefer a more political-inspired coinage, perhaps "Henotheism with Polytheistic Characteristics" :)

replies.title: Re: For the commentariat
replies.content: I had thought maybe something like "heirotheism" but my knowledge of Greek is limited to a few words that are points of dispute in Biblical exegesis between Protestants and Catholics.  Hierarchitheism doesn't look or sound worse than some other common Catholic/Christian words: Monarchianism, Chalcedonian, Trinitarianism, Nestorianism, Transubstantiation, etc.  So, if hierarchitheism is accurate Greek, I'm fine with it!

replies.title: Re: For the commentariat
replies.content: As soon as you said trunitarianism I personally just cringed. From my point of view it's just another form of modalism that christians fight over without realizing one can do both and get away with it rather easily. Kind of like henotheism or something like this heirarchy of God's. I'd say kriotheism or "Henotheist with polytheistic tendencies" works well enough to do the job but in my opinion I've kind of grown tired of the whole prescribing a label to every major difference in thought and action that otherwise didnt need to have a label.

replies.title: Re: For the commentariat
replies.content: I'm leaning strongly in the direction of kyriotheism.  As far as trinitarianism, it depends.  There are two basic approaches to explaining the Trinity.  There are many variations on those two approaches, but each tends to favor either modalism or tritheism.  If one takes the approach favored in Western Christianity, such as St. Thomas Aquinas or St. Augustine, it does tend to be a moderate form of modalism.  But some other approaches, particularly of the Social Trinity type, are more prone to becoming tritheism.  I used to favor the former approach but since becoming a polytheist, I tend to favor something along the lines of Social Trinitarianism.  William Lane Craig has some interesting work along those lines.

replies.title: Re: For the commentariat
replies.content: Jprussell's comments got me thinking -- what about kyriotheism? Kyrios in Greek means "first, preeminent, leading," and so it might work tolerably well for a view that considers one god to come first, and to be preeminent over the others.

replies.content: That seems like an apt description of my personal practice, so it would work rather nicely for me.  Thanks!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG,

1. Does the soul have astral vision in the after-death state? If so, how does the post-death astral vision compare to the astral vision that we enjoy now as incarnate beings?

2. Regarding the clause in the Neophyte oath stating “I vow that I will not suffer myself to be placed in such a state of passivity, that any person, power, or being may cause me to lose control of my words, thoughts or actions”, I understand that a classic interpretation of this passage is that the GD initiate is forbidden to place himself under hypnosis. Does the oath also exclude, e.g., getting drunk?

3. Still regarding the Neophyte oath, “I understand that there is nothing contrary to my civil, moral or religious duties in this obligation.” As a child, I was baptized into a church that claims spiritual exclusivity (the Orthodox Christian church). I don’t practice that faith and no longer identify as a member. However, would the baptism I received count among the “religious duties” that would prevent me from taking the Neophyte oath with a clean conscience?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yes, and it's much more vivid and steady in that state than it is when you're embodied. "But whilst this muddy vesture of decay doth grossly close it in, we cannot see it," to misquote Shakespeare.

2) Yes, if you mean drunk enough that you lose control of your thoughts, words, and actions. A little tipsy isn't necessarily a problem, but more than that? Not an option.

3) There are two factors at work here. First, to my mind, it's grossly abusive to claim, as some Christian churches claim, that a baptism performed for you without your consent or understanding, in infancy, imposes any form of obligation on you in later life.

Second, what that clause in the Neophyte oath means is that your Neophyte oath does not release you from any of your existing obligations; the promises you make as a Neophyte are all to be carried out, and all can be carried out, within the limits of your other duties. That word "duty" isn't an abstraction; it means specific things you've committed yourself to do, or not do. A Christian who becomes a Golden Dawn member (and of course a lot of the members of the original GD were Christians -- some were Anglican clergymen) is still obligated to pray, study the Bible, follow certain moral rules, attend church services, keep Advent and Lent, and so on; those are his or her duties as a Christian. Unless you took an oath saying "I will not practice Christian ceremonial magic," there shouldn't be a conflict.

comments.title: Red bag amulets
comments.content:
Dear JMG,
I am living in British Columbia, Canada and a lot of my friends were starting to suffer from the very unpleasant environment here. It is partly politically unpleasant and partly on a magical level with some crossover.

I was doing fine, with all my magical practices, and then I started to feel it too. I made up a red bag amulet but I also made a Duir talisman on a very small disc of paper and put that in my amulet. I also did this for the editor of the local paper as he was close to a breakdown.

Before I tied it around his neck, I explained that I had to tie three knots in the string and could do it in the name of the father, Son and Holy Spirit or any pantheon he liked but it had to be a three-fold blessing. He opted for the father, son and Holy spirit.

I was so surprised as I said the words and tied the knots, my whole body was flooded with tingling from my feet up. I didn't think those words still had any power but I was wrong. I have never done any Christian magic before as no one has asked for it.

Since I started wearing my talisman/amulet combination, I have felt fine and have been sleeping really well with it under my mattress. All my friends report feeling better and sleeping better from having their amulets. I plan to upgrade their amulets with Duir talismans when I have to refresh the amulets when they are 4 week old. The atmosphere is really bad here.

Maxine

replies.title: Re: Red bag amulets
replies.content: I'm glad to hear that the amulets are working well! That's interesting that the Duir talisman plays well with them -- that's a useful data point. The response you got from the invocation to the Christian god doesn't surprise me; the editor may have a good relationship with that deity.

replies.title: Re: Red bag amulets
replies.content: That's fascinating, thank you, Maxine! Could we have the exact recipe for that Duir talisman please? Can you use every Ogham sign for talismans?

replies.title: Re: Red bag amulets
replies.content:
Hi,
Yes, you can use every few to make a talisman and many other symbols too. The instructions for the talisman consecration are on pages 182 and 183 of, "The Druid Magic Handbook."

To perform this ritual successfully, it would be best to start at the beginning of the book and work through until you have all the background for the spell. It has been a long but worthwhile slog for me to get that far in the book. It may take you more or even less time to master the exercises but in any case, I found it to be very worth my time and efforts.

Until then, the red bag amulet is pretty good on its own.

My editor wrote this morning to say he is feeling a lot better.

Maxine

replies.title: Re: Saturn return
replies.content:
I just completed my second Saturn return, so yes, I may be able to offer some advice!

First of all, Saturn returns -- like void-of-course Moons and Mercury retrogrades -- have been turned into bugaboos by current pop-culture astrology, and that's inappropriate. How your Saturn return will work out depends on Saturn's condition in your natal chart. If it's essentially dignified and has good aspects, it can be a time of returning to basics, getting grounded, and laying down foundations for new growth, without any particular discomfort; if it's got difficult aspects or a less favorable essential dignity, it can still be these things, though there may be rough patches to get through.

So the most important thing to have in mind is that Saturn returns are not the end of the world. They needn't even be especially painful. Recognize that your limits will be stricter than usual at this time in your life, and that patience and perseverance are essential now, and you'll come through it just fine.

Since Saturn doesn't perfect the return in this pass, expect to have a preview of the issues that will be central to this return while Saturn comes within orb, but then those will fade out for a while before returning when Saturn goes direct again. That will give you additional time to prepare, and a chance to get a sense of what to expect.

comments.title: Past-Life Something.
comments.content:
Mr. Greer --

This week, I managed to join some separate experiences together. For a variety of stupid reasons, I never put all this together as being linked until this week. I ran through some divination on the matter, and it turns out that, yes, there is a tie there. What I'm going to do with this, how much I can learn, how much it matters, that I am still determining.

I am sending this out, not as a question exactly, but as an anecdotal data point. If you have opinions on it, feel free to let me know.

------------

When I was younger, I had a book on world mythology that included Siberian deities. When I read one entry, on the god Es of the Ket people, I found that it would trigger a mental image. In the image, I was floating over a landscape, brown with a grey river snaking through it that ran all the way to the horizon. There were mountains, and the sky was silvery-dark.

- It also felt like I remembered Es, although vaguely, like a friend you knew and lost contact with.
- When I started reading about tribal religions, it was like I remembered parts of what they were referencing, but that something about it was different, and I was so close to properly remembering what to actually do.
- When I listened to drumming, I could feel myself flying out of my body, I could see I was turning into a bird or a horse and flying, and it all felt so natural, like I was supposed to be doing that, that it was my job.
- When I looked at some of the old images, I felt like "oh, yeah, I remember wearing that"
It was like a photograph of yourself, where you didn't see it from that angle, but you can recognize the event.

(I put these things away. I believed that there was no connection, I had invented all of these experiences for bad reasons. Cut to 10 years later...)

Through a series of coincidences, I found myself looking up that childhood book, and the entry on Es, and then the people he belonged to, and where they were from.

When I searched for images of the Yenisei river, I actually let out a yelp, because I've seen that river in my dreams, often! Unfortunately, this was cut a bit short, because most of the images showed there being settlements along the river, all made of concrete, and I started feeling physically sick.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Gardener isn't really in Atkinson's league, but he's an interesting cat. He was one of the fixtures of the Los Angeles occult scene for a big chunk of the 20th century, and he's also the guy who published the original edition of The Eye of Revelation, the book that launched the Five Rites aka Five Tibetans into the world. (That's what brought him to my attention -- I'm currently busy on a research project in which the Five Rites are playing an increasingly central role.) He probably wrote most of the book himself, though there's evidence of someone else's work as well.

He fancied himself a prophet, and relied on the Great Pyramid as the basis for his prophecies, as a result of which nearly all his predictions were wrong. (Not quite Amazing Criswell levels of wrong, but still pretty solidly wrong.) On the other hand, his occult teachings are good solid New Thought-influenced American occultism, with a strong focus on learning to do your own thinking rather than absorbing your thoughts secondhand from society, and also on cultivating positive emotional states. You can find a good selection of his lessons here -- scroll down about halfway to find his name on the left.

comments.title: Tinsel, infinte blackness and birds
comments.content:
I posted in the Ecosophia comment section about my experience (almost?) dying after being vaccinated about a year ago. I had a Pfizer injection in the late evening April 8th, and at 6am April 9th I woke up to my, then, 1 year old crying to be fed. Sitting in the armchair nursing my son I felt how stiff the arm I'd gotten the injection in had become - I was using it to support my son's weight. Stiffness became a deep, sharp pain that felt as if the bone had been stabbed by the needle. I felt overwhelming nausea and yelled to my husband for help. That's the last thing I remember, but apparently I collapsed backwards, baby on safely on top of me.

The next thing I remember is being aware of something and not being able to make sense of what it was. I could see black, white and grey shapes, but did not seem to have any categories for them. I reached back into memory to try to figure out what had happened, and the only memory I had was of an infinitely dense shimmering blackness. This scared me, so I pulled my awareness back to the present and desperately searched for a way to make sense of the shapes I was seeing. I began to also hear as silence became buzzing became someone calling out a name. There was also the sound of a baby crying, and now I could make sense enough to see a face looming over me. It scared me even more that a baby was crying and I did not know why.

After a few more moments I remembered who I was and what must've happened. My husband pulled me up, but I returned the the ground on purpose (to prevent loosing consciousness again), and I asked him to call for paramedics, bring me some juice (in case it was blood sugar or dehydration), and hold our son - who had been quickly placed in his crib while my husband tried to wake me up.

While I lay there talking to my son and husband, I learned that I had been unconscious for approximately 30 seconds and had been breathing very abnormally: without pattern, and with a heavy, body racking snore - like a death rattle. I put my hand over my heart and it felt bigger than it ever has before. It could have been twice it's normal size. My heartbeat wasn't - a heart is supposed to beat in time but mine was flopping and shaking and had no discernible rhythm. I cried to release stress and prayed out loud.

An energy that was undeniably silver encircled my heart and made it feel very fuzzy. If I could try to describe it - like my heart had been taken inside a ball of soft, dense, Christmas tinsel. The tingling thing stayed at my heart for a number of minutes, then *slowly* moved down my vaccinated arm. I do not know what biochemical process could have moved at the pace at which this moved down my arm. It took about 10 minutes to start at my heart and progress down my arm. When it reached my hand it was over.

When the paramedics arrived they listened to my heart with a stethoscope, took my blood pressure, said I was healthy and that my vaccination the previous night was unrelated. I did not press to be taken the the hospital because I was scared, happy to be told I was OK, and wanted to stay with my son.

Okay - now onto the spiritual questions. I was born a Pisces sun, Gemini moon, Scorpio rising, and if I consider this to have been something akin to a quick dip in the abyss, then based on my death and rebirth date I am now an Aries sun, Pisces moon, Pisces rising. I am no expert in astrology, but from what I know the rising sign is thought to be the sign which you were in your past life, the moon sign your mother, and a Pisces, having completed one trip round the wheel, would start again as an Aries. I was due to be an Aries, but originally born 2 weeks prematurely due to my biological mother's ... habits. I interpret this as having given birth to myself and taken the path of becoming more who I might've been had my mother been an all around healthier person. Practically, I found the door to this choice available to me in a way it had not been before in my life, as soon after this experience I found the words cut ties with my biological mom.

From another angle, this occurred in time with the end of my Saturn return. I took the vaccine because my father asked me to, and it (almost?) killed me. I hero worshiped him throughout my life, and I'm not sure that anything else would have broken this spell and allowed me to see who he really is. He has never apologized, or acknowledged that I was harmed.

Interestingly, a few days prior to the event, I was returning home on a walk through the woods near where I then lived when a calm, jubilant voice spoke from somewhere within me. It said: "I am ready to die!"

It didn't really disturb me; an inner acceptance of death in a joyful, peaceful manner seemed healthy. But interesting that few days later I seem to have (almost?) briefly died.

I don't have a religious upbringing. I spent my working life studying and teaching mathematics. The Abyss reminds me of a vision of deep, empty space that I would mentally rest in when deep in study. It makes me think of possibilities waiting to exist out of the mind of God.

I would appreciate learning if, from a spiritual perspective, what I describe indicates that I died? Or something else?

In the days and weeks that followed I also had memories that were not mine - not big ones, not whole other lives - but strong, real memories of a lot of time spent picking berries, and one emotionally strong signature in which I am much older than I have ever been, there is something terrible happening, and I am taking care of a bunch of children, none of whom are mine. These seem like reasonable past life memories.

Thank your for these forums.
- Boo Da (my son's first, emphatic pronunciation of "bird")

replies.title: Re: Golden Section SOP
replies.content:
1) In the traditions of occult philosophy I follow, the Solar Logos is the source of all energy in the solar system, and the Planetary Spirit of the Earth (like the equivalent spirits of all the other planets) receives that energy and uses it to foster evolution among the beings that inhabit her material body. replies.title: Re: Personal spotlight
replies.content:
That’s a complex question. There’s two very different qualities that the term “charisma” is used to describe. One is our real or natural charisma, which is an inherent aspect of one of the parts of the Self. The part of our Selves that is the most embodied has a natural pull or influence, almost like a gravitational pull. Some have developed greater real charisma while others have less at this point in their trajectory, though it can be effectively trained and increased over a single lifetime. In fact, that’s one of the reasons we come into manifestation, to learn natural charisma, both across each lifetime and over the arc of all our lives. Real charisma leaves everyone energized as it is about bringing aspects of our souls closer together.

The other quality that we tend to label as “charisma” is the ability to enchant or ensorcel others. Rather than naturally attracting energy towards oneself, this kind of charisma pushes competing energies away. It charms its targets into not being able to look elsewhere or be distracted by anyone with real charisma. The narcissist described in the original post above was laying on this kind of charisma. This type leaves everyone else depleted, even though we may feel manically energized being around them at first.

Surprisingly, the disorder of the ensorceling charisma does not come from the embodied part of the Self with real charisma at all. The embodied part of the self has other disorders it can fall victim to. Ensorceling charisma is actually an imbalanced expression of the creative, imaginative part of the Self. Taken to its extreme it shows up as delusions of grandeur and schizophrenia. On a less extreme level it shows up as run-of-the-mill jealousy with all its component parts of keeping endless track of potential threats and punishing endless perceived betrayals, aka. “charming” its targets.

Are you asking about detoxing from a spotlight addiction in yourself or from someone else? Both involve letting go of jealousy, but for different reasons. If you are excessively practicing ensorceling charisma yourself, that habit has grown out of intolerable feelings of jealousy, so exhuming them and examining the remains is a healthy remedy. If you are trying to recover from too much exposure to someone else’s spotlight addiction, they have then dumped and projected their intolerable feelings of jealousy onto you, so shedding the jealousy they transferred onto you is the best medicine. The remedies are quite similar, but still subtly different. In both cases, allowing your real charisma to naturally draw what belongs in your life to you is equally useful, but a much more difficult practice.

— Christophe

comments.title: Choosing a Tradition
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

To keep things short since I’m low on time. I am currently having some trouble determining what tradition I should stick with (and actually I have been for a long time now).

I have always been a rather eclectic individual, reading from this-and-that philosopher, or praying to a variety of deities from different pantheons. But never once have I been able to comfortably settle down for very long.

What are some criteria that I can use to help me find myself a tradition which I can call home?

replies.title: Re: Choosing a Tradition
replies.content:
That's a common way that people protect themselves from learning new things. The mind is very uncomfortable leaving behind its old habits!

My advice? Choose a tradition at random from the ones you've encountered, and stick with it no matter what for one full year by the calendar. If you start tomorrow, keep going in that tradition until March 15, 2023. Of course you'll find fifteen million reasons why it's the wrong tradition for you -- that's your mind trying to weasel out of learning something new. Tell it to shut up, and keep doing the practices. At the end of a year, you can look back and decide whether to continue, or to try something else, because by then you'll have gotten past your mind's weasel-habits, you'll have learned some new things, and you'll have a clearer sense of what does and doesn't work for you.

Whatever you choose then, in turn, do for another full year!

replies.title: Re: Choosing a Tradition
replies.content: Thank you! I will give this strategy a try.

replies.title: Re: Choosing a Tradition
replies.content:
Not JMG, obviously, but I had your exact problem.

I have to say that I disagree slightly with JMG about the difficulty picking a path always being solely about the mind finding excuses to not do the work, because I have problems with second-guessing myself in ALL circumstances. I think my biggest problem on settling on a system was a genuine insecurity about making the wrong decision - as I do that with all sorts of things. Some of us are just more prone to self-doubting and second-guessing ourselves, imo.

Anyway, I asked Kimberly Steele to do an Ogham reading for me on which of the traditions I was considering was the one I should purse now to greatest benefit. Her Ogham gave an answer.

Was it the right answer? I hope so. But the important thing is, it was a way to make a decision. If I start wondering if it's the correct choice, I just remind myself that the Ogham said it was, so who am I to second guess the answer - and that clears up my doubting.

You might consider asking Kimberly for an Ogham reading on Sundays over on her Dreamwidth blog, or you can find someone else with divination experience to do a reading for you. I wouldn't recommend doing it for yourself; you'll just wind up second guessing your own answer. Get someone else to divine you an answer to the best path to pick, then stick with that answer for a year, as JMG recommended. By taking the burden of decision making off of yourself and surrendering it to the divination, you may find you have less trouble sticking with something.

That's my 2 cents, anyway.

replies.title: Re: Weekly Queries
replies.content:
1) The more hostile aspects in your natal chart, the more challenges you have to deal with and the greater your potential for strength. comments.title: Sigils and Tattoos
comments.content:
What are the differences between "sigils" and "seals", as they both seem to be used interchangeably in some cases?

Also, are any known side-effects or otherwise undesirable effects of crafting a sigil and having it tattooed on one's body? I've heard many conflicting opinions on this, with some close friends of mine claiming that there can be severe spiritual and even health consequences. Would you say this is true? Would the kind of sigil matter, as in a personal sigil vs. the sigil of a god, planet, or spirit?


As always, your curation of this space is greatly appreciated. Thanks once again!

Matt

replies.title: Re: Sigils and Tattoos
replies.content:
The words come from the same Latin root, sigillum -- in French that turned into sceau, and via the Normans that became "seal" in English. So, yes, they're often used interchangeably. The specialized use of "sigil" in magic is itself confusing, because it can mean a graphic image manufactured by AO Spare's method out of English letters, or a rather different image manufactured by a much older method from a magic square.

If you're going to create a sigil and then tattoo it on your body, be very, very sure you can deal with what it invokes, because you're stuck with it, and it'll manifest more forcefully in and around you than almost any other sigil. Yes, you can mess yourself over seriously if you don't pay enough attention to what you're doing. If you're even considering it, I'd recommend that you create the sigil and use it in other contexts for at least one year before going ahead with anything so permanent.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy Questions
replies.content:
1) Yes, you should close the account. Lines of credit are 8th house, and you have a mode of perfection between your significator in the 1st and the significator of the quesited in the 8th.

2) Of course. If you want to know if someone's trustworthy, that's 3rd house. If you want to know if someone else thinks you're trustworthy, go to the 3rd house from their house -- for example, if you want to know if your business partner thinks you're trustworthy, the partner is in the 7th, so the 3rd house from that is the 9th.
